title: Anomalous Mean-Squared Displacement in Quantum Active Matter from a Wigner Phase-Space Framework
authors: Sangyun Lee, Yehor Tuchkov, Alexander P. Antonov, Benno Liebchen, Hartmut Löwen, Giovanna Morigi, Michael te Vrugt
abstract: Active matter is driven out of equilibrium by a local influx of energy. While classical active matter has been extensively studied, the extension of active matter concepts to quantum systems has been explored far less. In this work we develop a full quantum description based on the Wigner function. By introducing a hybrid Wigner master equation that incorporates classical active motion and quantum degrees of freedom, we compute the quantum mean-squared displacement (MSD) using established techniques from classical active matter. We analytically derive the time dependence of the MSD and clarify the conditions under which the characteristic scaling with time $\mathrm{MSD}\sim t^{6}$ emerges. We further show that, for certain parameter and initial conditions, the MSD can exhibit an even steeper scaling regime $\mathrm{MSD}\sim t^{7}$, and we examine the robustness of these behaviors against quantum fluctuations of the initial state.
summary: This paper explores how the principles of active matter—systems driven out of equilibrium by local energy influx—apply to quantum systems. By using a Wigner phase-space approach, the authors show that quantum active matter can exhibit much faster, anomalous diffusion (scaling as $t^6$ or $t^7$) than classical active matter. They demonstrate that these high-order power-law growths are robust and depend on the interplay between quantum fluctuations and the persistence of the active noise. This work establishes a new framework for studying non-equilibrium dynamics in quantum-controlled environments.
main_problem: The paper investigates the extension of active matter concepts to the quantum regime, specifically focusing on the emergence and robustness of anomalous power-law scaling in the Mean-Squared Displacement (MSD).
main_result: The authors identify specific conditions for $t^6$ and $t^7$ anomalous scaling regimes in quantum active matter and demonstrate that these behaviors are robust against different initial quantum states.
method: A hybrid Wigner phase-space framework is developed, utilizing a hybrid Wigner master equation and the Moyal product to map quantum dynamics onto a quasiclassical Langevin equation.
model_or_system: A quantum harmonic oscillator coupled to an Active Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Process (AOUP), where the trap center follows a classical stochastic trajectory driven by active noise.
key_observables: Mean-Squared Displacement (MSD) and two-time correlation functions.
important_parameters:
Persistence time ($	au$), activity strength ($D_u$), harmonic frequency ($\omega$), dissipation/pumping rates ($
u_-, 
u_+$), and the squeezing parameter ($r$).
main_assumptions: The mapping to a Langevin equation assumes the Wigner master equation is quadratic in derivatives and the initial condition is Gaussian; the $t^7$ scaling is found under specific initial conditions.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows MSD trajectories for different persistence times; Figure 3 demonstrates $t^6$ scaling; Figure 7 compares different MSD definitions to show stability; Figure 8 compares three different numerical/analytical methods for calculating MSD; Figure 9 compares exact MSD results with asymptotic forms.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of quantum active matter, develops a hybrid Wigner phase-space framework, derives the master equation and its quasiclassical Langevin mapping, analyzes specific scaling regimes ($t^6, t^7$) and oscillatory dynamics, examines the impact of initial state squeezing, and provides mathematical proofs for the Moyal product and expectation value equivalence in the appendices.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous theoretical bridge between classical active matter and open quantum systems, offering new insights into how non-equilibrium driving can lead to extreme anomalous diffusion in quantum-controlled systems like optical tweezers.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Conceptual figure to illustrate the quantum active matter model from Ref. [23]. The Hamiltonian for this system is given by Eq. (18). The optical tweezer exert harmonic force on the quantum system, ˆρ. The computer generates activity term xc(t), and the center of the optical trap follows the generated trajectory Γc = {xc(t)}.

FIG. 2. Plot of MSD versus time of the quantum active particle with the initial conditions, Eq. (39). We varied Du, ω, ν−, and ν+ while fixing the ratio ν−to ν+ as 104. Two persistent time were considered: (a) τ = 0.1 case (b) τ = 10 case. The solid lines represent the MSDs with each parameter combinations. The dashed lines represent the MSD of xc(t). The same color coding is applied to both figures. All quantities in this and the following figures are shown in arbitrary units.

FIG. 3. Plot of MSD versus time of the quantum active particle with different Du. Du is varying from 0.1 to 106 Other parameters are chosen as follows: ω = 1.0, ℏ= 1.0, ν−= 10−4, ν+ = 10−8, and τ = 10. The dashed line represents tα where α = 5.9577... ± 0.00296. We set the mass and Planck constant to unity.

FIG. 4. Plot of MSD versus time of the quantum active particle with different persistent time and noise intensity for the activity. The blue solid line represents the case of long persistent time (τ = 1000) and high intensity (Du = 1000). The orange solid line represents the case of shorter persistent time (τ = 10) and lower intensity (Du = 10). The frequency of the quantum harmonic trap is chosen as ω = 0.08. The dissipation and pumping rates are ν−= 10−4 and ν+ = 10−8, respectively. We set the mass and Planck constant to unity.

FIG. 5. Plot of MSD [Eq. (36)] versus time of the quantum active particle with three initial conditions. The initial distribution for classical variables are chosen as Eq. (39). The orange ‘×’ marker shows the result of initially squeezed state [Eq. (48)] with r = 2.5 and the green ‘+’ marker shows the results of initially squeezed state with r = −2.5. The solid line represents the unsqueezed case. We set ω = 1, Du = 1000 and τ = 1000. The other parameters are same as that is used for plotting Fig. 4, for which the behavior scales as t6.

FIG. 6. Plot of MSD [Eq. (36)] versus time of the quantum active particle with three initial conditions. The initial distribution for classical variables are chosen as Eq. (45). The blue line represents the result for an initial ground state of a harmonic trap centered at xc = 0, which is the same result as in Fig. 4 (Du = 1000 and τ = 1000). The orange ‘×’ symbol represent the result with a squeezed initial state [Eq. (48)] of r = 2.5. The green ‘+’ symbol represents the result with squeezed initial state of r = −2.5. The frequency of the quantum harmonic trap is chosen as ω = 0.08. The dissipation and pumping rates are ν−= 10−4 and ν+ = 10−8, respectively. The persistent time τ for the...

FIG. 7. Plot of the difference between two moments, ⟨ˆx2(t)⟩xc −⟨ˆx2(0)⟩xc, versus time of the quantum active particle. The blue solid line represents the case of long persistent time (τ = 1000) and high intensity (Du = 1000). The orange solid line represents the case of shorter persistent time (τ = 10) and lower intensity (Du = 10). The frequency of the quantum harmonic trap is chosen as ω = 0.08. The dissipation and pumping rates are ν−= 10−4 and ν+ = 10−8, respectively. We set the mass and Planck constant to unity.

FIG. 8. Plot of MSD versus time of the quantum active particle calculated with three different methods. The blue line represents the result obtained by solving Eq. (G1). The orange line represents the result obtaind by numerically evaluating integral expression, Eq. (36). The cross symbol represents the analytically evaluated MSD; the explicit expression of MSD is omitted due to its complexity. The parameters all the same in Fig. 4 with Du = 1000 and τ = 1000.

FIG. 9. Plot of MSD versus time of the quantum active particle. τ = 1. The dash-dot line represents the asymptotic expression (Eq. (I1)). The hierarchy between the three characteristic times is set to be τ ≫1/ω, 1/γ.
